title: Mappings — :map, :noremap, and Friends
subtitle: How to bind a keystroke to a command in every mode, and what the modifiers actually do.
summary: Vim has one mapping system with a per-mode entry point. :nnoremap is the workhorse — non-recursive normal-mode map. <silent>, <expr>, and <buffer> are the three modifiers that cover 90% of real mappings.

blocks.text: A *mapping* binds a keystroke (or short sequence) to a command. It's how you build a personal layer on top of Vim's grammar — leader shortcuts, plugin bindings, language-specific helpers. The mapping system has more knobs than most people realize, and most of the bugs in handwritten vimrcs come from picking the wrong knob.
Mappings are scoped to a mode. Picking the right command (instead of bare `:map`) is the single most important habit.

rows: Bindings that act on the buffer from rest. The default for almost every leader mapping.
Live-while-typing shortcuts — autopairs, abbreviation-like helpers, completion triggers.
Rarely what you want; the Select half eats keystrokes meant as text. Prefer `xmap`.
Visual-mode bindings (e.g. `>` to indent and re-select).
Almost never. Select mode behaves like a text-replacing selection (think other editors).
Custom motions and text objects. After {key:d}/{key:c}/{key:y}, your map fires.
Bindings inside `:terminal` buffers (Neovim and newer Vim).
The bare form. Almost always too broad.
The other broad form. Sometimes useful for {key:Ctrl}-chords you want everywhere you type.

blocks.text: `map` vs `noremap` — the one you almost always want

Every mode has a `nore` variant: {key::}{key:n}{key:n}{key:o}{key:r}{key:e}{key:m}{key:a}{key:p}, {key::}{key:i}{key:n}{key:o}{key:r}{key:e}{key:m}{key:a}{key:p}, {key::}{key:v}{key:n}{key:o}{key:r}{key:e}{key:m}{key:a}{key:p}, etc. The `nore` prefix means *non-recursive*: the right-hand side is interpreted using Vim's built-in commands, not other mappings.

Without `nore`, your mapping can be re-mapped by another mapping. `:nmap j gj` then `:nmap gj jjj` will loop. With `:nnoremap j gj`, the `gj` on the right is the *built-in* `gj`, and the mapping is safe. **Use `nore` everywhere unless you have a specific reason not to.** "Specific reason" is almost always: you want to invoke another plugin's mapping deliberately.

rows: Don't echo the right-hand side on the command line as the mapping runs.
Almost always for mappings that call commands. Without it, the {key::}{key:s}{key:o}{key:m}{key:e}{key:c}{key:m}{key:d}{key:Enter} flicker is visible every keystroke.
Evaluate the right-hand side as a Vimscript expression; the *result* is what gets typed.
Conditional mappings — "if the popup menu is visible, do X; otherwise do Y." The classic `<expr>` use-case is wiring {key:Tab} for completion.
Mapping applies only in the current buffer. Stored on the buffer, gone when the buffer is wiped.
Filetype-specific mappings set from `ftplugin/`. Keeps your Python bindings out of your Markdown buffers.

blocks.text: Modifiers go *before* the keystroke, in any order:

blocks.title: The mapping-mode matrix
blocks.text:
Vim's documentation summarizes the system with a 7×8 matrix. Each row is a mode; each column is a `:map` command that fires in that mode. The diagonal you want is the *minimum-overlap* set:

```
              Cmd        Normal  Visual  Select  Op-pending  Insert  Cmdline  Term-job
              ---        ------  ------  ------  ----------  ------  -------  --------
  :map         (-)         •       •       •         •
  :nmap        n           •
  :vmap        v                   •       •
  :xmap        x                   •
  :smap        s                           •
  :omap        o                                     •
  :map!        (-)                                              •        •
  :imap        i                                              •
  :cmap        c                                                        •
  :tmap        t                                                                  •
```

Read it as: *which `:map` command fires in which mode*. Bare `:map` lights up four modes — that's why it's almost never the right answer. `:vmap` accidentally hits Select mode too, which is why `:xmap` (Visual-only) is what most people actually want. `:omap` is the only way to define a custom text object or operator-aware motion; it pairs with `:onoremap` and is the entire reason operator-pending (see [`advanced.op-pending`](#advanced.op-pending)) is exposed as its own mode in the first place.

The `nore` variants ({key::}{key:n}{key:n}{key:o}{key:r}{key:e}{key:m}{key:a}{key:p} etc.) occupy the same columns but disable recursive expansion of the right-hand side. Run {key::}{key:h}{key:e}{key:l}{key:p}{key: }{key:m}{key:a}{key:p}{key:-}{key:m}{key:o}{key:d}{key:e}{key:s} for the canonical version.

blocks.title: Useful read-only commands
blocks.text:
Same per-mode prefix, but without arguments lists every mapping in that mode:

  • `:nmap` — list every Normal-mode mapping.
  • `:nmap <leader>` — list every mapping that starts with your leader.
  • `:verbose nmap <leader>w` — show the binding *and* the file/line where it was last set. Indispensable for figuring out which plugin stole your shortcut.
  • `:map <buffer>` — list buffer-local mappings only.

In Neovim, the Lua spelling is:

```
vim.keymap.set('n', '<leader>w', ':write<CR>', { silent = true })
```

— same matrix, same modifiers (`silent`, `expr`, `buffer`, plus `desc` for which-key style helpers).

If a mapping "doesn't work," the first debug step is {key::}{key:v}{key:e}{key:r}{key:b}{key:o}{key:s}{key:e}{key: }{key:n}{key:m}{key:a}{key:p}{key: }{key:l}{key:h}{key:s}. It will name the file that defined whatever currently owns that keystroke — usually a plugin you forgot you installed.
